protocols.title: Discussion with Iga about Filling the Experimental Planning sheet:
steps: ### word/document.xml 11.6.2024 Discussion with Iga about Filling the Experimental Planning sheet: Sample ID slide will be the first sheet.

It should be machine readable , eg: no spaces, no extra characters no Capital letters.It will have information about the experiment panel that worked : eg: Experiment_1 or Experiment_2 or whichever is the final document.
Experimental Panel sheet named Experiment_X in which all the antibodies worked or the final one should always be the second sheet in our experimental planning.
Experiment_1 is the default name .If we repeat the experiment in case staining fails, we will have subsequent sheets with naming Experiment_2 , Experiment_3 etc.
The antibodies in experiment Panel will have the antibodies unique ID.
Unique antibody ID is given to each new vial even if the catalog number is the same.
However, if catolog number and lot number both are same then even different vial antibodies will have the same unique ID.
Column A named cycle Column C named antibody_id and column E named fluorochrome in Experiment panel sheet must be machine readable.
Information should be uniform, and no extra texts can be written in these columns.
It is researchers responsibility to fill all the columns in Experiment_1 sheet.

document_index.name: GeoMx & tCycIF Experiments Methodology.docx
document_index.title: GeoMx experiments
document_index.excerpt: ROIs from adjacent t-CyCIF images were selected based on five immune markers: CD4 (CD4 T cells, but macrophages can also express), CD8 (CD8 T cells), CD11c (DCs, but not restricted), Iba1 (macrophages) and NKG2a (NKs). The raw images of lower resolution (ZARR 3) were pre-processed. Firstly, a binary matrix for each marker was created based on the percentile threshold depending on the sample. Next, with the help of morphological operations, small noise was minimised bigger clusters of signals were created. The following procedure was used: Next, the overlap of pre-processed images between each pair, triplet, quadruplet and five of markers was found. Those images were then pre-processed to find bigger connected neighbourhoods, and ten ROIs were saved. These ROIs were then checked for overlap

document_index.name: geomx_methods_updated.docx
document_index.title: GeoMx ROI selection with lowplex t-CyCIF crop overlay
document_index.excerpt: The GeoMx spatial transcriptomics procedure was conducted at the Single Cell Analytics unit within the Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland (FIMM), adhering to the prescribed GeoMx guidelines for slide staining and scanning. The human Solid Tumor TME morphology marker kit was employed for this purpose. The tissue slide chosen to perform GeoMx sequencing was adjacent to the one on which lowplex t-CyCIF have been performed, to enable guided ROI selection. GeoMx ROI selection with lowplex t-CyCIF crop overlay Lowplex t-CyCIF stainings were employed to guide the selection of regions of interest (ROIs, n=160) in the GeoMx experiment. vector_chunks.source_file: 2. Methods and experiments/Discussion about experiment document and panel with Iga_11.6.2024.docx
vector_chunks.text:
### word/document.xml
11.6.2024 
Discussion with Iga about Filling the Experimental Planning sheet:
Sample ID slide will be the first sheet. It should be machine readable , eg: no spaces, no extra characters no Capital letters.It will have information about the experiment panel that worked : eg: Experiment_1 or Experiment_2 or whichever is the final document.
Experimental Panel sheet named Experiment_X in which all the antibodies worked or the final one should always be the second sheet in our experimental planning. Experiment_1 is the default name .If we repeat the experiment in case staining fails, we will have subsequent sheets with naming Experiment_2 , Experiment_3 etc. 
The antibodies in experiment Panel will have the antibodies unique ID. 
Unique antibody ID is given to each new vial even if the catalog number is the same. However, if catolog number and lot number both are same then even different vial antibodies will have the same unique ID.
Column A named 
cycle
 Column C named 
antibody_id
 and column E named 
fluorochrome
 in Experiment panel sheet must be machine readable. Information should be uniform, and no extra texts can be written in these columns.
It is researchers responsibility to fill all the columns in Experiment_1 sheet.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 2. Methods and experiments/GeoMx & tCycIF Experiments Methodology.docx::chunk_0000
vector_chunks.source_file: 2. Methods and experiments/GeoMx & tCycIF Experiments Methodology.docx
vector_chunks.text:
### word/document.xml
GeoMx experiments
1.
ROI selection 
ROIs from adjacent t-CyCIF images were selected based on five immune markers: CD4 (CD4 T cells, but macrophages can also express), CD8 (CD8 T cells), CD11c (DCs, but not restricted), Iba1 (macrophages) and NKG2a (NKs). The raw images of lower resolution (ZARR 3) were pre-processed. Firstly, a binary matrix for each marker was created based on the percentile threshold depending on the sample. Next, with the help of morphological operations, small noise was minimised bigger clusters of signals were created. The following procedure was used:
1)
Opening – 
kernel 10x10
2)
Closing – 
kernel 20x20
3)
Dilation – 
kernel 20x20
Next, the overlap of pre-processed images between each pair, triplet, quadruplet and five of markers was found. Those images were then pre-processed to find bigger connected neighbourhoods, and ten ROIs were saved. These ROIs were then checked for overlapping labels on cell interactions. Finally, ROIs were visually checked and picked in NAPARI. In total, we had ten ROIs per sample in Batch 2 and sample-dependent in Batch 3; in each sample, we attempted to collect as many diverse ROIs as possible. 
Only NKs cells did not exhibit hub structures. For them, either artificial or an overlap with Vimentin or PanCK was used. 
Details on ROIs can be found here: 
https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1DleeCF6K9YT_UZrccFOvEP7lZ773OtRcLMdoUFtLrYE/edit?usp=sharing
Final ROIs were then saved on the transformed PNG image.

GeoMx
All experiment procedures, details and corresponding notes can be found here: 
GeoMx_P9_EyeMTbatch3_P7_TLS_1.xlsx
Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue sections (5 µm) were sectioned adjacent to tCycIF slides. These sections are then processed according to the manufacturer’s instructions for the GeoMx Digital Spatial Profiler (DSP; Bruker Spatial Biology, MAN-10150-06). Briefly, slides were baked overnight, deparaffinized, and rehydrated before target retrieval in 1× Tris-EDTA (pH 9.0) using a steamer at ~99 °C, followed by Proteinase K digestion at 37 °C to expose RNA targets. Sections were then postfixed and hybridized overnight with the GeoMx RNA Probe Mix containing UV-cleavable oligonucleotide tags. After two stringent washes, customized morphology markers and nuclear stains were applied to enable ROI visualization.
Slides were scanned on the GeoMx DSP instrument to acquire whole slide fluorescence morphology images, and regions of interest (ROIs) were selected for illumination. Upon UV exposure, oligonucleotide tags were released from each ROI and aspirated into a collection plate for library preparation. Libraries were PCR-amplified using GeoMx Seq Code primers, pooled, purified, QCed and sequenced on Novaseq X 10B 100c following the GeoMx NGS Readout User Manual (MAN-10153).

FASTQ files were processed using the GeoMx NGS Pipeline to generate digital count conversion (DCC) files.

### word/document.xml
GeoMx
The GeoMx spatial transcriptomics procedure was conducted at the Single Cell Analytics unit within the Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland (FIMM), adhering to the prescribed GeoMx guidelines for slide staining and scanning. The human Solid Tumor TME morphology marker kit was employed for this purpose. The tissue slide chosen to perform GeoMx sequencing was adjacent to the one on which lowplex t-CyCIF have been performed, to enable guided ROI selection. 
GeoMx ROI selection with lowplex  t-CyCIF crop overlay
Lowplex t-CyCIF stainings were employed to guide the selection of regions of interest (ROIs, n=160) in the GeoMx experiment. The chosen regions contained tumor-stroma interface features, indicated by the presence of PanCK
+
 and Vimentin
+
 cells, and a variety of CD8 (CD8
+ 
T cells) and IBA1 (macrophages) staining markers for optimal ROI identification within the t-CyCIF crop (CD8
+
IBA1
+
, CD8
+
IBA1
-
, CD8
-
IBA1
+
, CD8
-
IBA1
-
). The ROIs were selected based on the relative abundance of CD8
+
 and IBA1
+
 cells observed within a given slide.  The chosen t-CycIF crop was imported into the GeoMx software and manually superimposed onto the GeoMx scan using  landmarks present in both images. Consistency in choosing similar types of areas, avoiding tissue folds, necrotic regions, or adipose tissue, while maintaining similarities in ROI size, tumor-stroma ratio and cell count across all samples was aimed. Each ROI was segmented into 2 distinct areas of Illumination (AOIs, n=320) separately for the tumor and stromal compartments. 
GeoMx and t-CyCIF data pairing
Whole slide image alignment was achieved in QuPath
100

count across all samples was aimed. Each ROI was segmented into 2 distinct areas of Illumination (AOIs, n=320) separately for the tumor and stromal compartments. 
GeoMx and t-CyCIF data pairing
Whole slide image alignment was achieved in QuPath
100
 through the Warpy plugin, which performs affine registration, incorporating the moving image to the coordinate system of the base image. The resulting files were exported as pyramidal ome.tiff files to be analyzed in other platforms. 
GeoMx data quality control and preprocessing.
DCC files obtained from the sequence provider were handled with the usage of GeomxTools R package
101
, provided by Nanostring. Segment (AOI) and probe level QC as well as LOQ (limit of quantification) filtering were performed as described in the package vignette. Overall, 
four AOIs
 and almost 2000 genes were removed during this procedure, leaving a total number of 310 AOIs and 16,727 genes detected in the whole dataset with sufficient quality. The median number of genes detected per AOI was 7911, and the median detection rate was 0.42.
GeoMx normalisation and batch effect correction
We’ve obtained a GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data from 16 HGSC samples (10 paired pre-post NACT treatment and 6 unpaired post-NACT treatment), described in the 
Launonen et.al 2024
 and deposited under 
https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672
. Data contains 160 regions of interest (ROIs) from tumor-stromal interface, segmented into independent tumor and stromal compartments (areas of illumination - AOIs, n=320). ROIs have been selected based on co-occurrence of CD8 positive T-cells and IBA1 positive macrophages. In this study we’ve utilized all tumor AOIs (n=160) regardless of presence or absence of CD8+/IBA1+ cells.

s (areas of illumination - AOIs, n=320). ROIs have been selected based on co-occurrence of CD8 positive T-cells and IBA1 positive macrophages. In this study we’ve utilized all tumor AOIs (n=160) regardless of presence or absence of CD8+/IBA1+ cells.
Since the data have been already subjected to the basic quality control
, we performed normalization using 
Deseq2
 followed by a variance stabilising transformation from the 
Deseq2 R package
. Batch effect correction has been performed using the 
harmony
 R package.
GeoMx signal deconvolution
Since GeoMx data for each AOI contains a mixture of signals derived from each cell type within TME, we performed deconvolution of the signal to obtain the cell-type specific transcriptional profiles. To do that, we utilized BayesPrism
55
 - designed for deconvolution of bulk RNAseq data, but validated on various spatial transcriptomics data, which apart from the fraction of cell types is able to compute the cell-type specific transcriptomics profiles. Since this method is based on reference scRNA data, we utilized the annotated ovarian cancer scRNA-seq dataset published under 
GSE266577
. 
We downsampled the full dataset in a patient-specific manner, generating the final dataset containing 24560 cells and 33458 genes. Due to the limitations of algorithms to correctly predict fractions of fine-grained immune cell types, we decided to cluster similar cell types into more general groups.

fic manner, generating the final dataset containing 24560 cells and 33458 genes. Due to the limitations of algorithms to correctly predict fractions of fine-grained immune cell types, we decided to cluster similar cell types into more general groups. Finally, as a reference for deconvolution algorithms, we used scRNA-seq profiles of: 9168 T cells (Regulatory T cells, Tcm/Naive helper T cells, Tem/Trm cytotoxic T cells, Type 17 helper T cells), 5240 tumor cells, 4364 macrophages (Classical monocytes, macrophages), 2335 fibroblasts, 1515 B cells (Memory B cells, Naive B cells, Plasma cells), 1007 NK cells (CD16
-
 NK cells, CD16
+
 NK cells, NK cells), 403 Dendritic cells (DC1, DC2, Migratory DCs, pDC), 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells and 151 ‘other’ cells (ILC, Late erythroid cells). 
For BayesPrism method we performed deconvolution according to their vignette (
see Key resources table
), filtering out low complexity genes from the downsampled scRNA-seq dataset, using above mentioned cell groups as ‘cell types’ and ‘cell states’ annotation (for cell states, tumor cells from each patient were annotated separately). As an input we used raw scRNA-seq and GeoMx counts, as suggested in the vignette.
GeoMx MHC-II pathway activity analysis and linear modelling
We’ve calculated a ssGSEA score of activity of MHC-II gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74) for the tumor AOIs, on full as well as tumor-specific de convoluted signal (obtained from BayesPrism), using GSVA
105
 R package.
Finally we examined the relation between ssGSEA score for MHC-II gene set and fraction of cell types (obtained by BayesPrism) in the same AOI.

full as well as tumor-specific de convoluted signal (obtained from BayesPrism), using GSVA
105
 R package.
Finally we examined the relation between ssGSEA score for MHC-II gene set and fraction of cell types (obtained by BayesPrism) in the same AOI. We fitted a basic linear model (y ~ exp(x)), followed by a model with added ‘Patient’ as a cofounder factor (y ~ exp(x) | Patient), given the fact that AOIs cannot be treated as independent observations.

Macrophages actively infiltrate tumor mass and are engaged in communication with tumor cells (vehicles such as CD9, endocytosis, APP). They get highly activated (division, translation, energy production) through IFN response, but it leads to high cellular stress and ER induced apoptosis/autophagy. Finally their response is reduced (IL-10 production). Similar, but less striking changes have been found in pre_NACT samples, but without APP on MHC.

main T-cell activity takes place in stroma, regulated in a complex manner (both pos and neg) by CCL5, LAPTM5, TGFb, and PTPRC. Stromal CD8+ Tcells have increased immunoglobulin recombination and production (TCR?), expression of MHCII molecules and are changing ECM through COL production mediated by TGFB. In contrary, pre_NACT stromal CD8+ T-cell are enriched in immunosuppressive signalling through TGFb and IL10RA

Similar differences are observed in pre_NACT samples, but without any MHC-II APP upregulation.

## Slide 99
BANKSY - Spatial PCA
BANKSY is a method for clustering spatial omics data by augmenting the features of each cell with both an average of the features of its spatial neighbors along with neighborhood feature gradients. By incorporating neighborhood information for clustering, BANKSY is able to
improve cell-type assignment in noisy data
distinguish subtly different cell-types stratified by microenvironment
identify spatial domains sharing the same microenvironment
BANKSY is applicable to a wide array of spatial technologies (e.g. 10x Visium, Slide-seq, MERFISH, CosMX, CODEX) and scales well to large datasets.

## Slide 118
Three types of simulations
CSR (complete spatial randomness):
All cell types generated independently
All cells placed randomly using poisson point process
Clustering amongst the cells of same type (based on Thomas process) :
Three cell type specific parameters:
Clustering probabilities
Cluster size ranges
Cluster spreads
Some cells become centers of clusters based on the clustering probabilities
There is possibility of nested clustering
(cluster inside cluster)
Interacting cell clusters:
Each cell type has specified affinity (probability score) towards other cells
When cell becomes a center of a cluster, the probabilities are used to determine the cell types of the cells in the cluster.

## Slide 159
Maija project ideas - data integration
Phase 1: primary approaches (would be great to assigned them as a focus of student)
1. Fully Integrated GAMM (that Iga proposed):
Fitting a single hierarchical model where ROI-level data is the dependent variable.
Challenge:
High risk of non-convergence or overfitting (capturing ROI noise instead of patient biology). A student can spend the time necessary to tune the smoothing parameters and random effects.
2. Two-step spatial phenotyping (Factor Analysis + GAMM):
Reducing spatial metrics into few interpretable ‘spatial immunophenotypes’ before modeling.
Challenge:
Risks information loss if two metrics with opposite biological effects are collapsed. The student can validate these factors against known biology to ensure we aren’t losing the signal.
Phase 2: Contingency models (If Phase 1 lacks power)
If the above approaches don’t yield stable results, I was considering following models as back-up:
Bayesian hierarchical modeling (via brms):
Excellent for handling uncertainty in small datasets, though computationally expensive and requires careful prior specification.
Geographically weighted regression (GWR):
Good for seeing how relationships shift across a slide, though it can lead to ‘fragmented’ results that are difficult to summarize at the patient level.
Phase 0:
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA):
Analysis of variables behaviour across dataset
Identifying crucial variables for future modelling
Scaling + normalising variables (to use them together in a given model)

Bergamo, Italy
Integration of highly-multiplexed imaging and spatial transcriptomics reveal contact-dependent differences in key immune-related molecular pathways and cell states in ovarian cancer 
Iga Niemiec
1
, Ada Junquera
1
, Erdogan Pekcan Erkan

, Matilda Salko

1) 
Research Program in Systems Oncology, Faculty of Medicine, University of Helsinki
2) Faculty of Medicine and Health Technology, Tampere University and TAYS Cancer Centre, Tampere, Finland 
3) Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center, Helsinki
University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland
4) Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, and Clinical trials unit, Comprehensive Cancer Centre, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland
5) Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland
6) iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine
The tumor microenvironment (TME) profoundly influences the response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC). CD8 T-cells and macrophages are among the most prevalent cell types within HGSC TME and understanding the interactions between them is essential for patient stratification and the development of novel combination therapies. The advent of spatial transcriptomics technologies enable comprehensive investigations of TME encompassing not only quantitative, qualitative, but also spatial dimensions.

or patient stratification and the development of novel combination therapies. The advent of spatial transcriptomics technologies enable comprehensive investigations of TME encompassing not only quantitative, qualitative, but also spatial dimensions.
This study investigates cell states within high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) samples' regions of interest (ROIs), categorized by CD8 T-cell and macrophage colocalization (CD8+macrophage+, CD8-macrophage+, CD8+macrophage-, CD8-macrophage-). Leveraging 11 post-NACT HGSC samples and multiplex fluorescent imaging (tCyCIF), we selected 111 of such ROIs. Subsequently, we identified the corresponding areas on adjacent slides and using whole transcriptome spatial transcriptomics (GeoMX) sequencing, we analyzed tumor and intratumoral stromal compartments' gene expression profiles. The dataset comprises 16,727 genes, with an average of 7450 genes per compartment.
For each of the 222 compartments, we assessed cell type composition, as well as immune-related signaling pathway activity, and cell states using gene set variation analysis (GSVA) and gene ontology (GO) enrichment analysis. Significant differences in certain immune-related pathways and cell states emerged between stromal and tumor compartments revealing higher overall immune activity in peritumoral stroma. Among ROIs with varied CD8/macrophage colocalization patterns, we’ve observed upregulation of PD1, CTLA4 signaling, as well as a shift towards M2 macrophages being more prevalent within CD8+macrophage+ ROIs. Notably, some changes, such as those within IFN-γ, IL2-STAT signaling, and MHC presentation, correlated positively with patients' progression-free survival.

as well as a shift towards M2 macrophages being more prevalent within CD8+macrophage+ ROIs. Notably, some changes, such as those within IFN-γ, IL2-STAT signaling, and MHC presentation, correlated positively with patients' progression-free survival. 
This study underscores the critical role of CD8+ T-cell–macrophage interaction in NACT response, unveiling contact-dependent molecular mechanisms that offer new therapeutic targets. By elucidating the dynamics of the TME, our findings contribute to personalized treatment strategies for HGSC patients.

The tumor microenvironment (TME) profoundly influences the response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC). CD8 T-cells and macrophages are among the most prevalent cell types within HGSC TME, but the growing evidence suggests that other immune cells such as dendritic cells (DCs), natural killer cells (NKs) and B-cells may be as much important for shaping anti-tumor response. Thus, studying the composition of TME and interaction between different cell types is essential for patient stratification and the development of novel combination therapies.

NKs) and B-cells may be as much important for shaping anti-tumor response. Thus, studying the composition of TME and interaction between different cell types is essential for patient stratification and the development of novel combination therapies.
This study investigates cell-type composition and cell states within HGSC samples' regions of interest (ROIs), categorized by CD8 T-cell and macrophage colocalization (CD8+macrophage+, CD8-macrophage+, CD8+macrophage-, CD8-macrophage-). Leveraging 16 pre and post-NACT HGSC samples and multiplex fluorescent imaging (tCyCIF), we selected 168 of such ROIs. Subsequently, we identified the corresponding areas on adjacent slides and using spatial transcriptomics (GeoMX) sequencing, we analyzed tumor and intratumoral stromal compartments' gene expression profiles. The dataset comprises 16,727 genes, with an average of 7450 genes per compartment.
For each of 
316 compartments
, we deconvoluted spatial transcriptomics signals to obtain the number of different cell types. We utilized two different methods (SpatialDecon and BayesPrism), and compared the results with the ground-truth information from cell-type calling on the corresponding region of adjacent tCycIF slide,which enabled us to obtain an accurate prediction of cell type fractions and cell-type specific transcriptional profiles. We found that the fractions of DCs, B-cells and NK cells correlate with the CD8+ T-cells–macrophages 
co-localisation 
patterns and change in response to NACT treatment. Additionally, we observed substantial differences in the activity of the key immune-regulatory pathways (such as IFN-γ, IL2-STAT signaling, and MHC presentation) in cell-type specific transcriptional profiles, in response to macrophages and T-cells presence as well as NACT treatment.

ial differences in the activity of the key immune-regulatory pathways (such as IFN-γ, IL2-STAT signaling, and MHC presentation) in cell-type specific transcriptional profiles, in response to macrophages and T-cells presence as well as NACT treatment. 
Overall our data suggest that DCs, B-cells and NKs are active players within TME and that their transcriptional profiles change with the proximity to other cell types. This study underscores the critical role of immune cells’ interaction in NACT response, unveiling contact-dependent transcriptional profiles that offer new potential therapeutic targets.

Tumor microenvironment (TME) significantly affects neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) response in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC). While CD8 T-cells and macrophages dominate within the HGSC TME, new research underscores the role of CD4 T-cells and dendritic cells (DCs) in shaping anti-tumor responses. Understanding TME composition and cell interactions is vital for patient stratification and novel therapy development.
We explored transcriptional cell states in HGSC, focusing on CD8 T-cell, CD4 T-cell, DC, and macrophage colocalization patterns. Analyzing 26 post-NACT HGSC samples with multiplex fluorescent imaging (tCyCIF), we identified 178 regions of interest (ROIs). Through spatial transcriptomics (GeoMX) sequencing of corresponding regions on adjacent slides, we analyzed gene expression in tumor and stromal compartments separately, generating two areas of illumination (AOIs) per ROI. Our dataset includes 18,122 genes, averaging 6,762 genes and a 0.37 gene detection rate per AOI.
We performed differential gene expression (DGE) analysis of AOIs with different immune cell colocalization patterns, followed by gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) of 10 661 MsigDB signatures. We found that key immune-regulatory pathways activation is connected with immune cell colocalization. Notably, 
IFN-γ and IL2-STAT signaling increased in stroma regions with CD8 T-cells and macrophages
, while MHC-II pathways were more active in tumor AOIs where CD4 or CD8 T-cells colocalize with macrophages or DCs, suggesting enhanced antigen presentation within cells infiltrating tumor mass.

ased in stroma regions with CD8 T-cells and macrophages
, while MHC-II pathways were more active in tumor AOIs where CD4 or CD8 T-cells colocalize with macrophages or DCs, suggesting enhanced antigen presentation within cells infiltrating tumor mass. Further ligand-receptor analysis via MultiNicheNetR on BayesPrism deconvoluted transcriptomics profiles, as well as gene network reconstruction using PyPanda and Lioness tools, identified critical interactions.
Our results highlight the importance of immune cell interactions in HGSC TME, revealing contact-dependent transcriptional profiles that deepen our understanding of the interaction dynamics of immune cells within TME.

Spatial multi-omic integration reveals molecular programs of chemotherapy-remodeled immune communities in ovarian cancer
 (120/120 characters)
Authors
: Aleksandra Shabanova 1, Iga Niemiec 1, Zhihan Liang 1, Saundarya Shah 1, Ada Junquera
1, Elias Ruuska 1, Ziqi Kang 1, 
Maija Tissarinen 1, 
Gayani Anandagoda 1, Anni Virtanen 1,3, Matilda Salko 1, Ulla-Maija Haltia 1,2, Anniina Färkkilä 1,2,3,4
1 Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland
2 Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology, Clinical Trials Unit, Comprehensive
Cancer Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland
3 Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center, Helsinki University
Hospital, Helsinki, Finland.
4 Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute for Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland
Introduction
Chemotherapy significantly changes the tumor immune microenvironment (TIME) in high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC). While immune aggregates can predict survival in treatment-naïve patients, their predictive value often diminishes after chemotherapy due to diverse, patient-specific mechanisms of immune evasion. We hypothesize that immune evasion is a complex process shaped by multicellular networks that determine functional states and drive resistance. By studying molecular mechanisms driving these patterns, this project aims to uncover novel prognostic markers and therapeutic targets, advancing personalized precision immunotherapies for HGSC patients.
Cohort & methods
We combined spatial proteomics (t-CycIF) and spatial transcriptomics (GeoMx) to analyze 58 HGSC samples from the Oncosys-Ova study, with 14 paired pre- and post-chemotherapy tissues.

lized precision immunotherapies for HGSC patients.
Cohort & methods
We combined spatial proteomics (t-CycIF) and spatial transcriptomics (GeoMx) to analyze 58 HGSC samples from the Oncosys-Ova study, with 14 paired pre- and post-chemotherapy tissues. A semi-automated pipeline facilitated GeoMx ROI selection by identifying overlapping immune marker signals in adjacent t-CycIF images. This workflow enabled targeted transcriptomic profiling of immune communities, with all samples linked to comprehensive clinical and sequencing data.
Results
In total, we quantified 6 distinct cell populations across the tumor microenvironment: macrophages, cancer, dendritic, CD8+ CD4+ T, and stromal cells, along with 1,180 whole-transcriptome segments. Using our in-house framework, SPACEStat, we modeled t-CycIF data as spatial cellular networks and quantified immune communities for all present immune cell combinations. Our framework also allowed us to integrate protein-level spatial architecture and molecular programs. We achieved high cross-platform correlation (R>0.65) in cell-type fractions, successfully mapping over 80% of transcriptomic segments to SPACEstat-detected immune communities from the TIME network.
Our integrated atlas reveals that post-treatment HGSC immune communities are highly patient-specific and are reshaped by chemotherapy relative to pre-chemo samples, with significantly greater spatial diversity in the post-treatment environment. By integrating these spatial immunophenotypes with clinico-molecular data via statistical modeling, we identified recurrent immunosuppressive tissue motifs linked to specific functional states.

ter spatial diversity in the post-treatment environment. By integrating these spatial immunophenotypes with clinico-molecular data via statistical modeling, we identified recurrent immunosuppressive tissue motifs linked to specific functional states. Ultimately, we demonstrate that spatial organization, rather than simple cell density, is the primary characteristic of the post-chemotherapy immune landscape in HGSC.
Conclusion
In conclusion, this study delivers precise spatial immunophenotyping, illuminating the context of immune cell interactions and their molecular programs. Our findings identify actionable targets and establish a foundation for precision immunotherapies in the post-chemotherapy landscape of HGSC.

Spatial Immunology, Computational Biology, Tumor Microenvironment, Spatial Multi-omics, Statistical Modeling

Combining these data layers with clinical and genomic information created a high-resolution map of the spatial molecular landscape in HGSC. Using our 
SPACEstat
 toolkit for spatial point pattern and network analysis, we identified highly patient-specific immune hubs shaped by diverse microenvironments. These findings highlight the heterogeneity of the tumor immune microenvironment and the need for integrated, multi-modal analyses.
Our ongoing work applies machine learning to consolidate spatial and molecular features, aiming to uncover immune vulnerabilities and inform the design of targeted immunotherapies for HGSC.

# EACR 2024
Analysis of spatial colocalization of CD8 T-cells and macrophages within high-grade serous ovarian cancer samples reveal contact-dependent differences in key immune-related molecular pathways and cell states.
Iga Niemiec
1
, Ada Junquera

, Erdogan Pekcan Erkan

, Anniina Färkkilä

2) Faculty of Medicine and Health Technology, Tampere University and TAYS Cancer Centre, Tampere, Finland 
3) Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland
4) Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland
5) iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine, Finland
CD8 T-cells and macrophages play crucial roles in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) tumor microenvironment (TME), and many studies have revealed their predictive role in determining patient response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT). Understanding the interactions between them is essential for patient stratification and the development of novel combination therapies. With the advent of spatial transcriptomics technologies, studying this interaction in terms of not only quantitative and qualitative, but also spatial aspects have become possible.
This study aims to investigate the cell states within regions of interests (ROIs) within HGSC samples, which differs in the colocalization of CD8 T-cells and macrophages (CD8+macrophage+, CD8-macrophage+, CD8+macrophage-, CD8-macrophage-).

possible.
This study aims to investigate the cell states within regions of interests (ROIs) within HGSC samples, which differs in the colocalization of CD8 T-cells and macrophages (CD8+macrophage+, CD8-macrophage+, CD8+macrophage-, CD8-macrophage-). To reach that goal, we utilized 11 HGSC post-NACT samples and using multiplex fluorescent imaging technology (tCyCIF), we identified ~10 ROI per sample, based on the presence or absence of CD8 T-cells and macrophages. Next, we identified the same areas on the adjacent slides and performed spatial transcriptomics (GeoMX) sequencing, separately for tumor and intratumoral stromal compartment.
Next, using deconvolution methods, we estimated the number of cells in each compartment, and estimated the activity of various immune-related signaling pathways and cell states using gene set variation analysis (GSVA) and gene ontology (GO) enrichment analysis. Our study revealed differences in the activity of many key immune-related pathways and cell states both between immune cells residing in the stromal vs tumor compartments, as well as ROIs with different CD8+/macrophages collocalisation patterns. Moreover, some of the identified changes have been positively correlated with the patients progression free survival (PFS). These results support the idea of the vital role of CD8+ T-cells – macrophage interaction in patients' response to NACT therapy and reveal the possible underlying molecular mechanisms, which may serve as potential targets for future therapies.

The composition and functional state of the tumor microenvironment (TME) play a crucial role in determining the response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) patients. Understanding these complex interactions is essential for patient stratification and the development of novel combination therapies. With the advent of spatial transcriptomics technologies, studying the TME in terms of not only quantitative, qualitative, but also spatial aspects have become possible.
This study aimed to investigate the spatial distribution and functional states of different cell types within the HGSC TME and their association with NACT response. 
We utilized 10 publicly available post-NACT HGSC 10x Visium spatial transcriptomics datasets comprising patients with good (n=3), partial (n=2), and poor (n=5) responses to NACT.

types within the HGSC TME and their association with NACT response. 
We utilized 10 publicly available post-NACT HGSC 10x Visium spatial transcriptomics datasets comprising patients with good (n=3), partial (n=2), and poor (n=5) responses to NACT. 
Each spot on Visium hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) slides has been manually annotated by a pathologist as tumor, mixed (both tumor and normal morphology), stroma or other. Three tumor-stromal interface (TSI) zones were defined based on proximity to tumor/mixed spots (TSI1 closest to the tumor, followed by TSI2 and TSI3).
Differential gene expression (DGE) analysis followed by overrepresentation analysis (ORA) was performed to characterize transcriptional pathway activity variations between tumor, TSI, and stromal spots. Notably, differential activity was observed in crucial pathways such as epithelial-mesenchymal transition, hypoxia, transforming growth factor beta (TGFb), and tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNFa) signaling, both within spot types and across different NACT responses.
Additionally, analysis of cell type distribution within the zones was conducted. UCell scores for HGSC TME cell type signatures were calculated for each spot, and cell type deconvolution was performed using the FindTransferAnchor method from the Seurat R package. Comparisons of mean UCell and deconvolution scores across spot types revealed substantial spatial variations in cell type composition, particularly for macrophages, fibroblasts, and plasma cells. Notably, these spatial distributions showed significant changes with the NACT response. For instance, macrophage scores were markedly higher in good responders and decreased with distance from the tumor. Fibroblast signatures were significantly elevated in good responders but only in

showed significant changes with the NACT response. For instance, macrophage scores were markedly higher in good responders and decreased with distance from the tumor. Fibroblast signatures were significantly elevated in good responders but only in 
close proximity
 to the tumor, while plasma cell scores were notably elevated exclusively within tumor and mixed spots of good responders. Furthermore, selected functional properties of these cell types were examined by computing UCell scores for crucial pathways governing their biology.
Collectively, our findings highlight substantial differences in TME composition depending on proximity to the tumor and NACT response. Macrophages, fibroblasts, and plasma cells exhibited the most prominent spatial patterns. These results underscore the importance of studying the TME within a spatial context to gain deeper insights into these intricate interactions, so important to the NACT response.

## Slide 1
Spatial patterns of immune cell states are associated with poor response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in high-grade serous ovarian cancer
Iga Niemiec1, Essi Kahelin1,2, Zhihan Liang1, Erdogan Pekcan Erkan3, Anna Vähärautio1, Anni Virtanen1,2, Anniina Färkkilä1,4,5,6
INTRODUCTION
ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
The composition and functional state of the tumor microenvironment (TME) play a crucial role in determining response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) patients. Understanding these complex interactions is essential for patient stratification and development of novel combination therapies. This study aimed to investigate the spatial distribution and functional states of different cell types within TME and their association with NACT response, in Visium spatial transcriptomics HGSC samples.
Our findings highlight substantial differences in TME composition depending on proximity to the tumor and NACT response. Macrophages, fibroblasts, T cells and plasma cells exhibited the most prominent spatial patterns. These results underscore the importance of studying the TME within a spatial context to gain deeper insights into these intricate interactions, so important to the NACT response.

CD8 T-cells and macrophages are considered the key players within HGSC TME, but other immune cells such as dendritic cells (DCs), natural killer cells (NKs) and B-cells may be as much important for shaping anti-tumor response. Understanding how the interactions between them changes through the course of NACT treatment is essential for development of novel combination

## Slide 1
INTRODUCTION
ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS & CONTACT
Tumor microenvironment (TME) profoundly influences the response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy and overall survival in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC)
.
CD8 T-cells and macrophages
are considered the key players within HGSC TME, but other immune cells such as
dendritic cells (DCs) and B-cells
may be as much important for shaping anti-tumor response. Understanding how the interactions between them are affecting
the TME
is essential for development of novel combination
therapies
.
Objectives of the
study:
identification of
immune cell types influencing key processes within HGSC
intratumoral stroma
analysing
how immune activity changes with the proximity to other immune cells
and identifying cell-type specific contact-dependent differences in activity of key transcriptional pathways
Multiple key cancer signatures are correlated with immune cell types abundance
in intratumoral stroma. As expected, these are
immune
signatures, but also some of the
proliferation and signalling signatures
.
Macrophages
cell type fraction is highly correlated with
elevated activity of key immune activation (MHC-I, MHC-II, complement) and pro-inflammatory signatures (IL-6-JAK-STAT3, inflammatory response, IFNg, TNFa)
, as well as the ones
promoting tumor growth and proliferation (p53, IL2-STAT5 and androgen response)
. It highlights the known
double-role
of macrophages in TME where, on the one hand - they elevate anti-tumor immune response and pro-inflammatory environment (M2-like), but on the other - promotes tumor growth and cause immunesuppression (M1-like).
B-cells
fraction is correlated with
tumor-growth promoting signatures - DNA repair and MYC targets, and negatively correlated with immune response signature

t (M2-like), but on the other - promotes tumor growth and cause immunesuppression (M1-like).
B-cells
fraction is correlated with
tumor-growth promoting signatures - DNA repair and MYC targets, and negatively correlated with immune response signature
, which may suggest their
immunosupressive role
within TME. Although mild negative correlation with TNFa is also visible.
Proximity to other cell types can change the activity of the identified pathways within cell-type specific deconvoluted transcriptional signal.
Macrophages pro-inflammatory signatures are significantly elevated in the presence of both CD8 T-cells (IFNg) and DCs (IFNg, IL6-JAK-STAT3)
, while the ones
promoting tumor growth (p53, androgen response, IL2-STAT5, TNFa) are higher when these cell types are less abundant.
That suggest that both CD8 T-cells and DCs may promote pro-inflammatory anti-cancer response of macrophages.
B-cells MYC targets signature is elevated with the higher proportion of DCs and lower proportion of macrophages
in the neighborhood, suggesting the opposed role of this cell types on pro-cancer activity of B-cells.

Tiivistelmä — Referat — Abstract

High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) is considered as the most common and deadliest
ovarian cancer subtype with poor prognosis. Currently available standard therapies do not effec-
tively improve patient survival, and due to the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME)
patients also respond poorly to immunotherapies. To understand these immunosuppressive mech-
anisms and to develop novel therapeutic strategies, it is crucial to understand the dynamic and
complex interactions between cancer cells and their microenvironment. One of the key approaches

unotherapies. To understand these immunosuppressive mech-
anisms and to develop novel therapeutic strategies, it is crucial to understand the dynamic and
complex interactions between cancer cells and their microenvironment. One of the key approaches
to decode these cell-cell communication patterns is to study ligand receptor interactions (LRIs)
by analysing their respective gene expressions with their spatial context. Among many spatial
transcriptomics methods, Geomx has recently gained popularity among scientists as a minimally
invasive high-plex technique which allows flexible selection of areas of interest within the TME.
However, there are currently no tools specifically available to predict ligands and their receptors
from these spatial data.

This thesis primarily focuses on developing a pipeline to predict and prioritize LRIs from spatial
transcriptomics data generated by the GeoMx Digital Spatial Profiler (DSP) adopting three widely
used tools ; BulkSignalR(designed for bulk RNAseq data), CellChat and NicheNet (both designed
for scRNAseq data). The pipeline is then used to predict LRIs within the stromal and tumor areas
of interest to identify spatially distinct communication between immune cells specifically focusing
on T Cells and Macrophages. From the predictions, we were able to prioritize 22 LR pairs based
on the LRI scores, statistical significance and expression regulation of LR genes, and among them
were well-established therapeutic LR pairs such as CD80/86-CTLA4 , PDCD1LG2-PDCD1. In
conclusion, the analysis showed that the pipeline is able to consistently predict LR pairs with
potential biological significance from GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data.

1. Introduction

High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) is recognized as the most lethal and
commonly occurring subtype of ovarian cancer, with a poor prognosis [11]. Currently
available standard therapies for HGSC include surgery, chemotherapy and the use of
Poly (ADP-Ribose) Polymerase inhibitors (PARPi), however all these strategies are as-
sociated with high relapse rates and limited survival outcomes [13]. As an alternative

ble standard therapies for HGSC include surgery, chemotherapy and the use of
Poly (ADP-Ribose) Polymerase inhibitors (PARPi), however all these strategies are as-
sociated with high relapse rates and limited survival outcomes [13]. As an alternative
for these methods, and also as an adjunctive treatment, immunotherapy approaches
have been investigated. Although clinical trials have shown promising results, unfor-
tunately many patients fail to respond to these treatments due to Immune Escape
Mechanisms (IEMs), which are not yet well understood in the context of ovarian can-
cer. New lines of evidence suggest that the Tumor microenvironment (TME) plays a
major role in the development of IEMs and shaping immunotherapy response [5]. TME
is a complex and dynamic system comprising not only tumor and non-malignant cells,
but also a wide range of different immune cell types. Therefore, understanding the
interplay between cancer cells and their microenvironment is essential for elucidating
tumor growth, progression, and therapeutic response. A wide range of molecules medi-
ate cell-cell interactions within TME, and these ligand-receptor pairs can serve as key
tools to decipher intercellular communication through the analysis of their respective
gene expression patterns in a spatial context.
 Spatial Transcriptomics (ST) is a collection of methods which allows for obtain-
ing transcriptomics information linked to the specific spatial coordinates of a tissue
sample. This is a very important advantage over bulk and single-cell RNAseq tech-
niques, where this information is lost, making it difficult to study highly organised
tissue ecosystems such as TME. Among different ST methods, GeoMx is particularly

sample. This is a very important advantage over bulk and single-cell RNAseq tech-
niques, where this information is lost, making it difficult to study highly organised
tissue ecosystems such as TME. Among different ST methods, GeoMx is particularly
interesting for TME studies, because of its high sensitivity and sequencing depth, as
well as flexibility, although it can provide only multi-cell resolution. GeoMx has been
used in studying the complex tumor microenvironment across various cancer types by
focusing on biologically relevant regions such as tumor-stroma interfaces, tertiary lym-
phoid structures, or areas defined by the presence or absence of specific tumor markers,
depending on the research objective. For an example a study by Launonen et al [12].
presented novel insights into tumor microenvironment and potential therapeutic tar-

gets for anti-tumor immunotherapies while demonstrating how GeoMx data, integrated
with other genomic and single-cell datasets, can be used in the context of HGSC giving
additional insight into complicated processes happening within its TME.
 Due to the fact that Geomx is a relatively new technology (first presented in
2020), currently, there are no established gold-standard methods for predicting ligand-
receptor pairs in these types of data. Most tools currently available for Ligand-Receptor
Interactions (LRI) analysis are designed for single-cell RNA sequencing data, with
few adapted for spatial transcriptomics data generated by nearly single-cell resolved
techniques like Visium, but not GeoMx. Consequently, establishing a validated pipeline
for detecting Ligand-Receptor (LR) pairs in GeoMx data is crucial for studying the
functional cellular interactions in the HGSC TME.

Aim and Objectives

This project aims to implement a ligand-receptor analysis pipeline as part of the down-
stream analysis of GeoMx data obtained from Formalin-Fixed, Paraffin Embedded
(FFPE) tumor samples of HGSC patients. Objectives of the project are,

 1. To implement existing Ligand-Receptor (LR) analysis tools; BulkSignalR,
 CellChat, and NicheNet, by adapting them to accommodate GeoMx data through
 necessary preprocessing specific to the tool’s requirements.

 2. To predict LRI separately within the tumor and stromal Areas Of Illuminations
 (AOIs), enabling the identification of spatially distinct cell-cell communication
 patterns within the HGSC tumor microenvironment.

 3. To prioritize predicted interactions by applying filters based on LRI scores, statis-
 tical significance, and expression regulation of LR genes, ensuring the retention of
 high confidence interactions that are consistently predicted across multiple tools.


Overview

This thesis is organized into 5 sections, including the current introduction. Section 2
provides the biological and theoretical background, discussing high-grade serous ovar-
ian carcinoma, the role of ligand receptor pairs and immunotherapy, GeoMx assay
and data analysis workflow, including BayesPrism tool as a method for cell type de-
convolution. The third section describes the materials and methods used, including
sample information, data preprocessing, and tools utilised during the analysis. Section
4 presents the results, covering LR analysis pipeline schema, the outcomes of ligand

2. Background

2.1 High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC)
Ovarian cancer is the third most common gynecological malignancy, the seventh most
common cancer overall, and the eighth leading cause of cancer related deaths among
women worldwide [13][19]. Among its various subtypes, High-grade serous ovarian car-
cinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and by far the deadliest, accounting for 70-80%
of all ovarian cancer related deaths [13]. HGSC is characterized by somatic mutations
of the p53 coding gene TP53 and genomic instability due to defects in DNA repair
pathways and copy number alterations [19]. Due to the absence of specific early symp-
toms and vague clinical presentation, HGSC is rarely detected at an early stage, with
more than 60% of cases diagnosed at an advanced stage [13][19]. It typically exhibits
rapid dissemination of malignant cells throughout the abdominal cavity. Lack of re-
liable biomarkers for early detection and insufficiency of current screening methods
further hinder early diagnosis and over 80% of patients in the advanced stage experi-
ence recurrence, which ultimately results in disease related mortality [19].
 The primary treatment for advanced HGSC involves Primary Debulking Surgery
(PDS) followed by multiple cycles of chemotherapy. For patients with extensively

d stage experi-
ence recurrence, which ultimately results in disease related mortality [19].
 The primary treatment for advanced HGSC involves Primary Debulking Surgery
(PDS) followed by multiple cycles of chemotherapy. For patients with extensively
metastasized disease, Neo-Adjuvant Chemotherapy (NACT) is administered prior to
Interval Debulking Surgery (IDS). Moreover, for patients with Homologous Recombi-
nation repair Deficiency (HRD), Poly (ADP-Ribose) Polymerase inhibitors (PARPi)
are used as targeted therapy. However, innate and acquired resistance to PARPi re-
mains a major clinical challenge. To exacerbate the situation, immunotherapies, which
show promising outcomes in certain cancer types, are often ineffective due to an im-
munosuppressive TME frequently dominated by tumor-associated macrophages that
hinder effective anti-tumor immune responses [15].
 Tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex and organized ecosystem compris-
ing cancer cells surrounded by various types of nonmalignant cells, all embedded in
a highly vascularized extracellular matrix [6]. These nonmalignant (mainly immune
and stromal) cells, once considered mere bystanders, are now known to play critical
roles in cancer pathogenesis [6]. For example Launonen et al. [12] demonstrated that

 4

through myeloid-T cell interactions mediated by the NECTIN2-TIGIT ligand-receptor
axis. This interaction is strongly associated with poor chemotherapeutic response. The
study also highlights NECTIN2-TIGIT as a promising target for the development of
novel immunotherapeutic strategies aimed at restoring anti-tumor immunity and im-
proving treatment outcomes.

trongly associated with poor chemotherapeutic response. The
study also highlights NECTIN2-TIGIT as a promising target for the development of
novel immunotherapeutic strategies aimed at restoring anti-tumor immunity and im-
proving treatment outcomes. This highlights the important role of immune cells within
TME for HGSC progression and chemotherapy response and the need for investigat-
ing their communication, especially in the spatial context, to find new immunotherapy
approaches.


2.2 Ligand Receptor pairs in Cancer Immunother-
 apy
The importance of receptors in cancer was first recognized in 1971 with the discovery
of dysregulated estrogen receptors in breast cancer patients [14]. Today, one of the
most extensively studied ligand-receptors in tumor research are immune checkpoints.
They are ligand-receptor interactions that inhibit excessive activation of immune cells
to protect normal tissues from immune-mediated damage. The first immune checkpoint
receptor identified was Cytotoxic T-Lymphocyte-Associated Antigen 4 (CTLA-4) [14].
Since then, numerous other immune checkpoint receptors have been discovered and
investigated for their therapeutic potential in cancer treatment over the past decade.
 Foundation of cancer immunotherapy lies in the ability of the human immune
system to identify and eliminate malignant cells. In cancer, malignant cells often
generate neoantigens, which are recognized by the immune system, leading to the ac-
tivation of T cells and the development of cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) that have
the ability to detect and destroy tumor cells. However, cancer cells can evade this
immune response by employing so-called Immune Escape Mechanisms (IEMs) e.g. by
exploiting immune checkpoint pathways. They achieve this by upregulating inhibitory

ve
the ability to detect and destroy tumor cells. However, cancer cells can evade this
immune response by employing so-called Immune Escape Mechanisms (IEMs) e.g. by
exploiting immune checkpoint pathways. They achieve this by upregulating inhibitory
ligands on their surface, which suppress T cell activity and facilitate immune evasion,
tumor progression, and metastasis. This understanding led to the development of Im-
mune Checkpoint Blockade (ICB), a therapeutic strategy that targets these inhibitory
pathways to restore T cell activity and enhance the immune system’s ability to elimi-
nate cancer cells. Clinically approved immune checkpoint targets include Programmed
Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1), Programmed Death 1 (PD-1), and Cytotoxic T-Lymphocyte-
Associated Antigen 4 (CTLA-4) [7].
 Although ICB has demonstrated the ability to enhance antitumor immunity, in
certain cancer types such as HGSC, there is a limited efficacy, with no compelling overall
6 Chapter 2. Background

response rates observed [7]. As a result, ongoing efforts are focused on identifying
additional immune checkpoints to improve therapeutic outcomes. In parallel, research
is also directed toward understanding alternative immune escape mechanisms employed
by cancer cells.


2.3 GeoMx spatial transcriptomics technique

Spatial Transcriptomics (ST) analysis provides spatially resolved transcriptomic data
by linking gene expression profiles to their tissue and cellular locations. This spa-
tial context enables the study of organization and communication of diverse cell types
within complex tissue environments, insights that cannot be fully captured by single-
cell RNA sequencing (scRNAseq) alone. Based on how spatial information is cap-

al context enables the study of organization and communication of diverse cell types
within complex tissue environments, insights that cannot be fully captured by single-
cell RNA sequencing (scRNAseq) alone. Based on how spatial information is cap-
tured and recorded, ST methods are classified into three main categories: in situ
hybridization-based methods, spatial DNA-barcoding techniques, and region selection-
based approaches [9].
 Among these different ST approaches GeoMx Digital Spatial Profiler (DSP)
(DSP) has recently gained significant popularity among researchers, for several com-
pelling reasons. This advanced platform is considered as a nearly whole transcriptome
level spatial transcriptome method, which allows flexible selection of Regions Of In-
terests (ROIs) within the tissue [9]. Importantly, it achieves this with minimal tissue
destruction, allowing for subsequent analyses on the same sample to extract additional
information. GeoMx is particularly advantageous in limited sample settings and is
compatible with both Formalin-Fixed, Paraffin Embedded (FFPE) and frozen tissue
specimens, making it versatile for clinical and research applications. The downside of
this method is that its resolution is far from single-cell, since the minimum number of
cells detected in one ROI is 100, while typical ROI contain around 100-300 cells.
 The NanoString GeoMx DSP is a tissue-based platform developed for high-plex
profiling of proteins and RNA within specifically defined Regions Of Interests (ROIs).
The profiling workflow presented in Figure 2.1 follows a five-step process: (1) Tissue
preparation and staining using immunofluorescence biomarkers and dsp probes, (2)
Selection of ROIs guided by biomarker expression and possible division for multiple

e profiling workflow presented in Figure 2.1 follows a five-step process: (1) Tissue
preparation and staining using immunofluorescence biomarkers and dsp probes, (2)
Selection of ROIs guided by biomarker expression and possible division for multiple
Areas Of Illuminations (AOIs), (3) Collection of oligonucleotide tags from each AOIs,
(3) Hybridization and counting of transcript-specific barcodes to generate expression
data, and (4) Data analysis to interpret spatial gene or protein expression patterns [9].

2.4 GeoMx data analysis

As a result of the GeoMx DSP pipeline, full-transcriptome expression information for
each selected region in the sample (AOI) can be obtained. Transcriptomics profile
of each AOI is a mixture of all transcripts from all cells within this area (typically
100-300), which effectively make it a ’mini-bulk’ RNAseq data, although leveraging
additional information of tissue location and cell composition.
 After undergoing quality control and normalization, these spatial transcriptomic
profiles can be used for downstream analyses. Furthermore, since the expression signal
obtained from AOI is a mixture of all cell types found within it, the data can be decon-
voluted to estimate both cell-type proportions and cell-type-specific gene expression
profiles, enabling a more granular understanding of the cellular composition within
each AOI.
 Cell deconvolution refers to the process of inferring cell type proportions from
bulk RNA sequencing data. Among the various tools developed for this purpose,
BayesPrism is recognized as a particularly robust approach. It employs a Bayesian
framework based on statistical marginalization to estimate both the cell type fractions

m
bulk RNA sequencing data. Among the various tools developed for this purpose,
BayesPrism is recognized as a particularly robust approach. It employs a Bayesian
framework based on statistical marginalization to estimate both the cell type fractions
and cell type-specific gene expression from bulk RNA-seq data. BayesPrism utilizes
prior information derived from the reference single-cell RNA-seq dataset to inform its
model, enabling it to jointly infer the posterior distribution of cell type composition
and gene expression in each sample [4].
8 Chapter 2. Background


2.5 Ligand Receptor analysis
Ligand-Receptor (LR) interaction analysis is widely performed in-silico using gene ex-
pression data derived from bulk RNA-seq, single cell RNA-seq, or spatial transcrip-
tomics. Numerous computational tools such as BulkSignalR, NicheNet, and CellChat
have been developed to infer LR interactions based on these data types, each employing
different strategies to predict intercellular communication. However, there is currently
no dedicated tool for LR analysis tailored to GeoMx Digital Spatial Profiling data.
The only available workaround is to treat GeoMx output as bulk-like data for LR in-
ference, or to deconvolute the data and apply single-cell tools, both approaches that
unfortunately discard the critical spatial context provided by GeoMx. This limitation
highlights a significant gap in the field and underscores the need for specialized tools
that can leverage the spatially resolved, yet region-aggregated nature of GeoMx data
for meaningful LR analysis.

across 16 FFPE slides derived from 11 high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) patients.

3. Materials and Methods

3.1 Data
This project utilizes spatial transcriptomic data generated from 310 AOIs (after QC)
across 16 FFPE slides derived from 11 high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) patients.
The tissues are sampled from patients with varying clinical outcomes, including five
patients with data collected both before and after chemotherapy treatment. Each AOI
consists of 100-200 cells. The final dataset comprises 16,727 genes, with an average of
7450 genes per AOI.


3.2 Method
Two different approaches will be employed to analyze GeoMx data for ligand-receptor
pairs: (1) full expression profiles of the GeoMx data, and (2) deconvoluted cell-type
specific transcriptional profiles derived from the full signal.


1. Bulk RNAseq-based approach
To get an overall insight into ligand receptor activity, BulkSignalR [20], a novel tool
which is specifically designed to detect ligand-receptor pairs using bulk RNA-seq data,
is used. Therefore the pipeline uses the full transcriptomic signal of the Geomx data
which is a mixture of signals typically derived from 100-300 cells.


3.2.1 BulkSignalR
Scripts to run the BulkSignalR analysis on Geomx data are written following the tool’s
vignette. A BulkSignalR object is first constructed using the prepareDataset() function
from the BulkSignalR package, applying its default Q3 (Quantile 3) normalization
method.

 9
10 Chapter 3. Materials and Methods

Ligand receptor prediction

BulkSignalR predicts ligand receptor interactions by generating null distributions to
assess the significance of Spearman correlation coefficients between a ligand and a
receptor and between the receptor and its target genes. Initially, BulkSignalR gen-

SignalR predicts ligand receptor interactions by generating null distributions to
assess the significance of Spearman correlation coefficients between a ligand and a
receptor and between the receptor and its target genes. Initially, BulkSignalR gen-
erates randomized expression datasets and the mean expression of each gene across
all samples is calculated. Genes are then grouped into bins based on their expression
levels, with a default bin size of 20. Within each bin, the order of genes is randomly
shuffled. The shuffled gene order is then used to construct the randomized expression
matrix.This randomized expression matrix generation is repeated 5 times. Spearman
correlation coefficient is calculated across the samples, for each Ligand-receptor pair
that matches with the LRdb (Ligand receptor database from SingleCellSignalR Bio-
conductor package) and also repeated for each generated random datasets to construct
the gene correlation matrix needed to generate final ligand receptor null model / distri-
bution. Among the three candidate models; censored normal, censored mixture of two
normals, and Gaussian kernel based empirical, BulkSignalR selects the null distribution
that best fits the randomized data. Same null model is used for the receptor target
null model as well.To control for multiple testing, Benjamini-Hochberg correction is
applied using the multtest R package, which is the default method in BulkSignalR

Best Pathway Selection

Spearman correlation and p value for all ligand receptor pairs are calculated and cor-
related target genes in the pathways downstream the receptors are identified. The
Spearman correlations are then ordered in increasing magnitude. For each target gene,

man correlation and p value for all ligand receptor pairs are calculated and cor-
related target genes in the pathways downstream the receptors are identified. The
Spearman correlations are then ordered in increasing magnitude. For each target gene,
the probability of observing it within the 0.75th percentile of correlation values relative
to all other genes in the pathway is calculated to assess significance of that gene to be
a target. These downstream target genes are annotated using reactome pathways or
the GO terms.

Visualization: Pathway score per sample

Each target gene is normalized separately across samples to obtain z-scores, and the
activity of Ligand receptor interaction on the sample (signature score) is calculated by
averaging these z-scores of all the target genes of the ligand receptor pair.
3.2. Method 11

2. scRNAseq-based approaches
In this approach we’re aiming to use the tools designed for scRNAseq data; CellChat
and NicheNet, since previous studies have demonstrated their utility in analyzing de-
convoluted GeoMx data [3][8]. Therefore, deconvoluted cell type specific transcriptomic
profiles calculated using the BayesPrism tool as described in the Launonen et al. [12]
are used in investigating ligand-receptor interactions, with a particular focus on im-
mune cell types of interest. Before any type of preprocessing, AOIs with low quality
predictions by BayesPrism have been removed from the expression matrix.


3.2.2 CellChat
Scripts to run the CellChat analysis on Geomx data are written following the tool’s
vignette[4].


Data Preprocessing

Cell chat needs to be provided with a library size normalized expression matrix. There-
fore the pipeline takes deconvoluted cell-type-specific transcriptomic profiles which are

ata are written following the tool’s
vignette[4].


Data Preprocessing

Cell chat needs to be provided with a library size normalized expression matrix. There-
fore the pipeline takes deconvoluted cell-type-specific transcriptomic profiles which are
not variance stabilized.Since the CellChat tool expects a scRNAseq expression ma-
trix as an input, we mimic it by combining all deconvoluted cell-type specific profiles
for all AOIs and all cell-types. For library size normalization of the data we used
estimateSizeFactors() function from Deseq2 library. The combined metadata ma-
trix for all cell types was constructed using the metadata from the Geomx object.
Normalized count matrix is log2 transformed with a pseudocount of 1. Finally the
cells are filtered out based on cell fraction values calculated by the BayesPrism tool
for each cell type. As each AOI consists of min 100 and max 300 cells, we have tested
both 1 percent and 0.5 percent cell fraction cutoff threshold. Pipeline can be also run
with no filtering based on cell fraction.


Ligand receptor prediction

In our pipeline, we used CellChat v2[10] to analyze cell-cell communication. A CellChat
object was created using the createCellChat() function, with the combined expres-
sion matrix and combined metadata as inputs. For ligand-receptor interaction analysis,
we employed the CellChatDB database for humans, excluding the "Non-protein Sig-
naling" category to focus on protein-mediated communication.

3.2.3 NicheNet within MultiNicheNet framework
Scripts to run the NicheNet analysis on Geomx data are written following the tool’s
vignette. The Ligand receptor prediction by NicheNet pipeline includes three steps:
(1) Data preprocessing, (2) Ligand receptor prediction, and (3) Visualization.

(1) Data Preprocessing

In the Preprocessing step, AOIs are filtered based on cell fraction values from the
BayesPrism cell type de-convolution.

(2) Ligand receptor prediction

NicheNet prior model

NicheNet predicts cell-to-cell communication using a prior model built from integrated
knowledge of cell signaling and gene regulatory networks.This process involves four key
steps: (1) Network construction, (2) Signaling importance score calculation, (3) Score
thresholding, and (4) Construction of the ligand-target matrix. First, the tool builds a
ligand signaling network based on ligand receptor interactions and signal transduction
information from different data sources. Then a matrix of signalling importance scores
is built by applying Personalized PageRank (PPR) on the ligand-signaling network
selecting each ligand as seed node [2]. The PPR score vector v for a given ligand is
computed as:

 v = (1 − d) × u + d × W × v (3.5)

where:

v = signaling importance score

d = damping factor (probability of not returning to the seed node)

u = personal preference vector (1 for seed ligand, 0 otherwise)

W = normalized adjacency matrix of the weighted ligand signaling network.
14 Chapter 3.

= signaling importance score

d = damping factor (probability of not returning to the seed node)

u = personal preference vector (1 for seed ligand, 0 otherwise)

W = normalized adjacency matrix of the weighted ligand signaling network.
14 Chapter 3. Materials and Methods

 The iterative process continues until convergence of v. The score matrix is then
adjusted so that, for each ligand, target genes with scores below a specific quantile
threshold are set to zero, allowing only highly enriched target genes to retain a nonzero
importance score. Finally this matrix is multiplied by the weighted adjacency matrix of
the integrated gene regulatory network to obtain the ligand-target matrix of regulatory
potential scores.


Ligand-receptor analysis

In our pipeline, we use NicheNet-v2 [2], the updated and significantly enhanced version
of NicheNet’s ligand-receptor interaction network and ligand-target prior knowledge
model (ligand-target matrix).
 Ligand-receptor analysis begins by defining the sender and receiver cell types.
Potential ligands are identified by intersecting the genes expressed in the sender cells
with the ligands listed in the ligand receptor interaction network. This set can be
further refined by selecting only those ligands whose corresponding target genes are
also expressed in the receiver cells.
 A gene set of interest must be provided to the tool, depending on the biologi-
cal question to be addressed. This would generally be differentially expressed genes
between two groups or conditions of interest in the receiver cell population. The devel-
oped pipeline allows the user to provide the gene set of interest, as well as the expressed
ligands and receptors, as preferred. Inside the Nichenet’s "predict_ligand_activities"

or conditions of interest in the receiver cell population. The devel-
oped pipeline allows the user to provide the gene set of interest, as well as the expressed
ligands and receptors, as preferred. Inside the Nichenet’s "predict_ligand_activities"
function, Area Under Precision Recall Curve (AUPR) is calculated for each ligand by
comparing the expression patterns of the target genes of the ligands in the prior model
with the provided gene set of interest. A target gene is considered a true positive if
it appears in both sets. All the other expressed genes in the receiver cell which are
not differentially expressed, also known as the background set of genes by the tool,
are used to calculate the true negatives and false negatives. Adjusted AUPR is calcu-
lated by subtracting the fraction of positives from the AUPR. Positive adjusted AUPR
values denote that prediction is better than a random prediction. All the ligands are
ranked based on these adjusted AUPR scores. As for the top most prioritized ligands
we selected the top 50 and also 250 associated top target genes were identified based
on their regulatory potential scores from the ligand-target matrix.


(3) Visualization

For Better visualization we used NicheNet within MultiNicheNet framework [1] as it
allows us better comparison between conditions of interests. It extends the NicheNet
prioritization which is done solely based on the ligand activity score(adjusted AUPR

scores), by calculating a prioritization score considering the following four criteria as
listed in the tool’s vignette [1].

 • Upregulation of the ligand in a sender cell type and/or upregulation of the re-
 ceptor in a receiver cell type - in the condition of interest

a prioritization score considering the following four criteria as

 • High NicheNet ligand activity, to further prioritize ligand-receptor pairs based

 Inside MultiNicheNet, differential gene expression analysis is done by EdgeR after
calculating pseudo bulk expression per sample. Users of the pipeline can either allow
multinichetr to calculate differentially expressed genes or provide their own differen-
tially expressed gene analysis results as a .csv file
 As for the top most prioritized ligands we selected the top 50 pairs and also 250
associated top target genes were identified based on their regulatory potential scores
from the ligand-target matrix.


3.3 Code Availability
The code used to perform LRI analysis on GeoMx data is written in R and is available
on GitHub https://github.com/farkkilab/geomx-processing.git.

In this project, we aimed to apply various ligand-receptor prediction tools to the GeoMx
spatial transcriptomics data, test their applicability and validate by comparing their
predictions. A schematic representation of the custom ligand-receptor analysis pipeline
developed for GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data as a main result of this Master
Thesis, is shown in Figure 4.1. As described in section 3, data processing was performed
separately for MultiNicheNet and CellChat to meet the specific input requirements of
each tool. It is recommended to use differentially expressed genes (DEGs) calculated
using a linear mixed model as an input for NicheNet and this data are calculated in
the main GeoMx processing pipeline developed in Färkkilä laboratory and provided in
a .csv file format.
 After running our own analysis in the pipeline we did a comparison between each
tool’s approach as well as their strengths and weaknesses in analyzing Geomx data for
ligand target prediction, as shown in the table 4.1.

4.3 Cell type resolved LR interactions
Analysis with BulkSignalR gave us an important overview of the overall immune com-
munication landscape in tumor and stromal AOIs. Nevertheless, to examine differences
between activities of specific immune cells which already infiltrated tumor mass versus
those which stayed in the nearby stroma, we need to use tools which allow us to study
cell types separately. CellChat and NicheNet are popular LR prediction tools designed
for scRNAseq data. Our GeoMx data is not single-cell resolved, but divided to AOIs
containing 100-300 cells, but we can mimic scRNAseq dataset by performing AOIs
deconvolution by BayesPrism, to obtain cell type specific transcriptomics profiles (this
deconvoluted profiles have been calculated as a part of main Geomx pipeline developed
in Färrkilä laboratory). As a result, for each AOI we obtain a cell-type specific sample.
Using this data, we decided to examine the cell-type specific transcriptional profiles
of Macrophages and T cells - the key cell types within HGSC TME, to examine how
they communicate between each other and whether these patterns differ between cells

sing this data, we decided to examine the cell-type specific transcriptional profiles
of Macrophages and T cells - the key cell types within HGSC TME, to examine how
they communicate between each other and whether these patterns differ between cells
found in tumor and stromal AOIs.


4.3.1 CellChat
CellChat allows users to predict ligand-receptor pairs by identifying highly variable
expressed genes between cell types of interest (T cells and Macrophages). From this

probabilities using the expression of ligand in the sender cell samples and the expression
of the receptor in the receiver cell samples, as previously described in the Methods

 These calculated probabilities and their p-values can be used to select the most
probable ligand receptor pairs for each condition/group of interest which are in our case
stroma and tumor. Using the thresholds of p-value < 0.01 and probability > 0.05, we
were able to select a total of 30 ligand-receptor pairs in between T cells as the sender
and Macrophages as the receiver (Supplementary Figure A.2). In both stromal and

Figure A.2). The most probable LR pair in stromal AOIs is HLA-A - CD8A with a

 Chemokine CCL5 has been consistently identified as an important ligand by
both CellChat and BulkSignalR tools. It is expressed by T cells and communicates
with CCR1 and CCR5 receptors on macrophages. Among these, the CCL5-CCR5
ligand-receptor pair is particularly noteworthy, as it is considered a promising thera-
peutic target in cancer which is also considered to be associated with cancer progres-
sion as well [18]. In addition, the CD86-CTLA4 ligand-receptor pair is also predicted
by both BulkSignalR and CellChat and represents the well-known mechanism of T
cell suppression in TME [16]. Other ligands consistently identified across analyses
include Tcell stimulating SELPLG, HLA-A, HLA-F and ICAM1, highlighting compli-
cated relation between T cell activation and suppression signalling within the tumor
microenvironment. Within the LR pairs identified by CellChat only, we can find e.g.
CXCL16-CXCR6, IFNg and TGFb representing other key immune signalling pathways
of HGSC.
