4.3.2 NicheNet LR prediction in cell-type specific transcrip-
 tional profiles with MultiNicheNet framework

As previously explained in the Methods section, NicheNet requires a gene set of interest
to prioritize potential ligands. The selection of this gene set depends on the specific
biological question being addressed. Therefore, In our analysis, to define the gene set
of interest, the differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between stromal and tumor AOIs
for each deconvoluted cell type specific transcriptomic profile (Tcells and Macrophages)
needed to be calculated.
 For GeoMx data, the recommended method for differential expression analysis is
the use of a linear mixed model (LMM), with sample IDs included as a random effect
to account for inter-sample variability and to control for confounding due to sample
specific differences. MultiNicheNet framework also performs its own differential expres-
sion analysis using edgeR, a state of the art method for analyzing multi-sample single
cell RNA-seq data which supports the inclusion of fixed-effect covariates through gen-
eralized linear modeling. Therefore to investigate differences in prioritization patterns
between the two methods described above, we calculated DEGs using both approaches
and prioritized ligand-receptor pairs accordingly. In the MultiNicheNet framework,

zed linear modeling. Therefore to investigate differences in prioritization patterns
between the two methods described above, we calculated DEGs using both approaches
and prioritized ligand-receptor pairs accordingly. In the MultiNicheNet framework,
sample ID was included as a fixed effect covariate to control for tissue specific variabil-
ity across samples.
 A comparison of the predicted results revealed that many ligand-receptor pairs
were consistently prioritized across both approaches. Therefore, for subsequent analy-
ses, we proceeded with the results based on LMM-based DEG list calculated externally
based on the same dataset, as part of a main GeoMx processing pipeline, while top
50 prioritized ligand-receptor pairs, predicted using DEGs identified by edgeR, are
presented in Supplementary Figures A.6 and A.7.
 As previously described in the Method section, NicheNet prioritized ligands based
on its prior model and provided DEGs between conditions of interest in the receiver
cell population. Then Multininichenet prioritized ligand receptor pairs based on four
criterias. For prioritization MultiNicheNet calculates the product of the expression
levels of the ligands in the receiver cell population and its corresponding receptor
(taken from the Nichenter’s ligand receptor interaction network) across all samples
for each ligand-receptor pair. To facilitate visualization, we computed the median of
these scaled product values separately for stromal and tumor AOIs. The difference
in medians between the two groups was then plotted as shown in the bubble plots
in Figure 06 and to assess the statistical significance of this difference, a Wilcoxon
rank-sum test was performed.

4.4 Results Summary
We have tested three different computational tools (BulkSignalR, CellChat and Nich-
eNet) for ligand-receptor predictions, to identify the most probable immune Tcell-
Macrophages communication patterns within tumor and intratumoral stroma com-
partments. Despite the fact that each tool had different underlying methodology and
input data type, we were able to find certain ligands and receptors which have been
not only consistently identified by multiple tools, but also known to play an important
role in HGSC TME.
 The combined outputs of BulkSignalR, CellChat, and MultiNicheNet are sum-

, we were able to find certain ligands and receptors which have been
not only consistently identified by multiple tools, but also known to play an important
role in HGSC TME.
 The combined outputs of BulkSignalR, CellChat, and MultiNicheNet are sum-
marized in Table 4.2, providing a comprehensive overview of consistently identified
ligand-receptor pairs (ligands and receptors are separately available in supplementary
tables A.1); their direction of communication, and their expression in stromal and tu-

5. Discussion and Conclusion

Ligand receptor (LR) analysis offers valuable insights into cell cell communication in

5. Discussion and Conclusion

Ligand receptor (LR) analysis offers valuable insights into cell cell communication in
different types of transcriptomics data. It enables researchers to have an insight into
direct cell cell interactions, which is especially important to study immune related phe-
nomenons such as the ones occurring in the tumor microenvironment. Unlike other
spatial transcriptomics technologies, GeoMx enables the selection of specific regions
of interest (ROIs) within tissue sections, but it lacks single-cell resolution. Instead,
it captures the aggregate gene expression from all cells within each ROI, effectively
generating a "mini-bulk" transcriptomic profile encapsulating signal from 100-300 cells.
Consequently, deconvolution is necessary to estimate the relative contributions of differ-
ent cell types as well as cell type specific transcriptomics profiles, prior to downstream
analyses such as LR inference. Despite these limitations, GeoMx gives an unprece-
dented opportunity to study cell-cell interactions in the spatial context, which is not
possible in either bulk nor scRNAseq. Due to this, we believe that implementing and
validating a pipeline for LR analysis for GeoMx data is an important opportunity to
obtain a unique insight into a cell’s microenvironment by studying cell-cell communi-
cation patterns in a spatially resolved manner.
 Currently, no LR analysis tools are specifically designed for GeoMx data. Re-
searchers often re-use methods originally developed for other types of data, adapting
them to the new context and carefully validating. Following this approach, in our anal-

rrently, no LR analysis tools are specifically designed for GeoMx data. Re-
searchers often re-use methods originally developed for other types of data, adapting
them to the new context and carefully validating. Following this approach, in our anal-
ysis, we employed tools designed for both bulk and scRNAseq data and identified a
set of ligands and receptors that were consistently predicted, regardless of the method-
ological differences. This cross tool consistency reinforces the biological relevance of
the identified interactions.
 The aim of this project was to adapt three commonly used LR prediction tools:
BulkSignalR (designed for bulk RNAseq data), CellChat and NicheNet (both designed
for scRNAseq data), to GeoMx data, compare the results, identify their limitations
and find out whether they produce a biologically-relevant results. To achieve this, we
performed an analysis aiming to describe LR landscape in tumor and intratumoral
stromal compartments of HGSC TME, specifically focusing on interactions between
Tcells and Macrophages

 31
32 Chapter 5. Discussion and Conclusion

 Our results suggest that NicheNet, within the MultiNicheNet framework, is par-
ticularly effective at discerning condition specific LR interactions. However, because
it focuses on differentially expressed target genes of ligand receptor signalling, it may
miss interactions that have equal affect across all conditions.
 Some ligand-receptor pairs may not be predicted by certain tools simply because
they are not present in the underlying databases those tools use. For example, the
ALOX5AP-ALOX5 and CCL13-CCR5 interactions were predicted by both NicheNet
and BulkSignalR, but not by CellChat. Upon inspection, this ligand-receptor pair is
absent from the CellChat database.

he underlying databases those tools use. For example, the
ALOX5AP-ALOX5 and CCL13-CCR5 interactions were predicted by both NicheNet
and BulkSignalR, but not by CellChat. Upon inspection, this ligand-receptor pair is
absent from the CellChat database. However, recent versions of CellChat allow users
to customize and update the database by adding their own curated ligand-receptor
interactions prior to performing predictions. This flexibility enables the inclusion of
biologically relevant interactions that may otherwise be missed due to database limi-
tations.
 As expected from our analysis, notable differences in ligand-receptor (LR) inter-
actions were observed between immune cells in the tumor and stromal compartments,
with many key immunoregulatory pathways predicted to be active. Well-established
therapeutic LR pairs such as CD80/86-CTLA4 , PDCD1LG2-PDCD1 were among the
predicted interactions. Additionally, LR pairs currently under investigation for their
immunotherapeutic potential such as LILRB4-LAIR1, ALOX5AP-ALOX5, and the
receptor CCR5 were also identified. Of particular interest is the CCL13-CCR5 inter-
action, which may play an important immunomodulatory role within the tumor mi-
croenvironment. Further validation using multiplex imaging technologies like tCyCIF
or transcriptomic approaches such as GSEA could elucidate the spatial organization
and functional relevance of these interactions, supporting their potential as targets for
future immunotherapy strategies
 In summary, our analysis shows that it is possible to use tools designed for bulk
and scRNAseq data to analyse LR in GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data. By using
multiple tools with different underlying methodologies we were able to obtain a subset

In summary, our analysis shows that it is possible to use tools designed for bulk
and scRNAseq data to analyse LR in GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data. By using
multiple tools with different underlying methodologies we were able to obtain a subset
of consistently predicted LR pairs with probable biological significance, which we can
use for further analysis of HGSC TME interactions. Nevertheless our analysis was
limited and several other tools remain unexplored in this context, such as CellPhoneDB
and ICELLNET, which are designed for single RNA-seq data. Although these have
not yet been reported to be used in GeoMx-specific analyses, their documentation
indicates they could potentially be adapted for L-R inference in bulk RNA-seq data
derived from pure cell populations and consequently for cell type specific deconvoluted
GeoMx data. Exploring these tools may offer further opportunities for uncovering
biologically meaningful interactions in spatial transcriptomics data.

### word/document.xml
Project 9 - EyeMT 
Multiomics (tCycIF ‘proteomics’, GeoMx spatial transcriptomics on the adjacent slide and WES genomics) data integration for understanding the role of different immune cell types (mainly T-cells and myeloid cells) and interactions between them in shaping cancer immune escape mechanisms and the overall outcome of the patients

Gayani Anandagoda
 (ligand-receptor analysis of geomx data)(11.2024-05.2025)

B. ROI Integration Principles
For integration analysis, interaction hubs and ROIs should be at the same scale -> Action: SPACEStat tweaking?
The threshold for network interaction must be a tradeoff between the resulting cell count and the number of hubs, optimizing for downstream statistical analysis.
Collect ROIs that do not overlap with network interaction hubs and check them visually. In theory, these could serve as negative controls to investigate background activity.

README.md 2026-05-26


 Project 9 EyeMT
 Project Overview
 Integrating multiomics data to understand the role of diverse immune cell types—specifically T-cells and
 myeloid cells—and their spatial interactions in shaping cancer immune escape mechanisms and overall
 patient clinical outcomes.
 Core Data Modalities
 Proteomics: Cyclic Immunofluorescence (tCycIF)
 Spatial Transcriptomics: GeoMx (performed on adjacent tissue slides)
 Genomics: Whole Exome Sequencing (WES) via iCAN database
